coredata.dc:title: Amphiphilicity-adaptable graphene quantum dots to stabilize pH-responsive pickering emulsions at a very low concentration
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Rong
Zeng, Minxiang
Huang, Dali
Wang, Jenny
Cheng, Zhengdong
Wang, Qingsheng
coredata.dc:description: Hypothesis: Stimuli-responsive Pickering emulsions have attracted considerable interest due to their widespread potential applications. Especially pH-responsive behavior could be easily implemented. In this work, we reported a pH-responsive Pickering emulsion based on amphiphilic graphene quantum dots at a low concentration which shows a great potential from the environmental and economic perspective. The stimuli responsive properties would make the smart Pickering emulsifiers recyclable and reusable. Experiments: The amphiphilic-adaptable graphene quantum dots functionalized by alkyl groups (C-GQDs) were synthesized by a facile one-step pyrolysis method. The pH-responsive emulsion performances were investigated, and the mechanism of pH-responsive of C-GQDs was studied by dynamic light scattering. Findings: The amphiphilicity of C-GQDs could be acquired controllably and effectively by this facile one-step pyrolysis method, which are able to stabilize Pickering emulsion at a very low concentration (0.001%). The amphiphilicity of C-GQDs are capable of changing in response to environmental stimuli. When the pH value of aqueous solution adjusts to 2, these C-GQDs aggregate in contrast to their stability in neutral condition due to the alternation of surface charges. The pH-responsive aggregation/ dispersion behavior of C-GQDs allows us to tune the interactions between oil-in-water emulsion droplets without introduction of destabilization agents. This will provide huge economic benefits in industrial applications in the future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: pH-responsive
Carbon-based surfactant
Pickering emulsion
Low concentration
Recyclable

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of C-GQDs based Pickering emulsion
Reversible emulsion behavior induced by pH

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: PetroChina Scientific Research and Technology Development Project

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The author Dr. Z. Cheng would like to acknowledge financial support from PetroChina Scientific Research and Technology Development Project. The authors acknowledge use of the Materials Characterization Facility (MCF) and Microscopy and Imaging Center (MIC) at Texas A&M University. The authors also thank Dr. Mustafa Akbulut for using DLS instrument.

The author Dr. Z. Cheng would like to acknowledge financial support from PetroChina Scientific Research and Technology Development Project. The authors acknowledge use of the Materials Characterization Facility (MCF) and Microscopy and Imaging Center (MIC) at Texas A&M University. The authors also thank Dr. Mustafa Akbulut for using DLS instrument.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of one-step synthesis process of C-GQDs.
(a) FTIR and (b) XPS survey spectrum of pure GQDs and C-GQDs; High-resolution XPS spectrum of (c) C1s and (d) O1s peaks from amphiphilic C-GQDs.
(a) TEM image and (b) AFM image of C-GQDs. Inset graph presents the height profile along the indicated line. The contact angle measurement of (c) unfunctionalized GQDs and (d) functionalized C-GQDs.

(a) Optical micrographs of dodecane-in-water emulsion stabilized by 0.001% C-GQDs. (b) The DLS spectra of emulsion droplet distribution of Pickering emulsion stabilized by C-GQDs. Inset: Photograph of emulsion stabilized by C-GQDs.

(a) Photographs and optical micrographs of C-GQDs based dodecane-in-water emulsion under different pH values. (b) Size and (c) zeta potential of C-GQDs in aqueous solution under different pH values. (d) Schematic illustration of C-GQDs surface charged change by tuning pH.

Photographs of C-GQDs based Pickering emulsions: (a) the reversible demulsification-emulsification cycle induced by changing pH; (b) reuse of C-GQDs as emulsifier, in which removing initial oil and adding new fresh oil.

head.ce:title: Amphiphilicity-adaptable graphene quantum dots to stabilize pH-responsive pickering emulsions at a very low concentration

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Artie McFerrin Department of Chemical Engineering, Texas A&M University, College Station, TX 77843, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Artie McFerrin Department of Chemical Engineering, Texas A&M University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Artie McFerrin Department of Chemical Engineering, Texas A&M University, College Station, TX 77843, USA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Material Science & Engineering, Texas A&M University, College Station, TX 77843, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Material Science & Engineering, Texas A&M University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Material Science & Engineering, Texas A&M University, College Station, TX 77843, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hypothesis: Stimuli-responsive Pickering emulsions have attracted considerable interest due to their widespread potential applications. Especially pH-responsive behavior could be easily implemented. In this work, we reported a pH-responsive Pickering emulsion based on amphiphilic graphene quantum dots at a low concentration which shows a great potential from the environmental and economic perspective. The stimuli responsive properties would make the smart Pickering emulsifiers recyclable and reusable.

Experiments: The amphiphilic-adaptable graphene quantum dots functionalized by alkyl groups (C-GQDs) were synthesized by a facile one-step pyrolysis method. The pH-responsive emulsion performances were investigated, and the mechanism of pH-responsive of C-GQDs was studied by dynamic light scattering.

Findings: The amphiphilicity of C-GQDs could be acquired controllably and effectively by this facile one-step pyrolysis method, which are able to stabilize Pickering emulsion at a very low concentration (0.001%). The amphiphilicity of C-GQDs are capable of changing in response to environmental stimuli. When the pH value of aqueous solution adjusts to 2, these C-GQDs aggregate in contrast to their stability in neutral condition due to the alternation of surface charges. The pH-responsive aggregation/ dispersion behavior of C-GQDs allows us to tune the interactions between oil-in-water emulsion droplets without introduction of destabilization agents. This will provide huge economic benefits in industrial applications in the future.

ce:section.ce:para: The Pickering emulsions produced by amphiphilic colloidal or inorganic nanoparticles have received considerable interests due to their important role in a large number of industrial processes and products [1,2]. In Pickering emulsions, solid nanoparticles absorb at the interface of two immiscible phases, forming a nanoparticle film to prevent coalescence [3–5]. Many nanomaterials have been developed to stabilize Pickering emulsions, such as zirconium phosphate [6], functionalized clay nanoparticles [7], and polymer particles [8]. Compared to the conventional molecular surfactants, less nanoparticles are required to produce stable Pickering emulsions [9]. Moreover, conventional organic surfactants may impose environmental concerns. Therefore, nanoparticles used for Pickering emulsions can be considered eco-friendly and cost-saving emulsifiers [4]. Pickering emulsions have a broad range of applications due to long-term stability, such as food science [8], cosmetic formulations [10], and oil & gas industries [11]. However, emulsions with tunable stability may enables advanced applications, such as controlled release [7], emulsion polymerization [12], interfacial catalysis [13]. Therefore, emulsions which could be switched between long-term stability and rapid demulsification have attracted enormous attention. Smart Pickering emulsions are stabilized by nanoparticles with stimulus-responsive properties, which can switch the emulsion system stability through appropriate environmental triggers including electricity [14], temperature [15], addition of electrolytes [3], pH value [16], or magnetism [17]. Among these stimuli methods, the pH-responsiveness of Pickering emulsions is one of the most readily implementable responses [10].

Carbon-based nanomaterials have attracted much attention in the past decades owing to their low cytotoxicity, biocompatibility, cost-effectiveness, and facile surface modification [5,18]. With amphiphilic chemical groups and structures, carbon nanomaterials could possess huge potential for Pickering emulsion applications. He et al [4] prepared a Pickering emulsion using graphene oxide (GO), which could achieve good stability at low pH conditions. When the pH value adjusts to 1, the emulsion fraction reached its maximum volume. In addition, the stability of the GO-stabilized emulsion could be affected significantly by the type of oil and addition of salt. Moreover, properties of nanoparticles play a major role in Pickering behaviors as well, including the wettability, shape, size and concentration [4]. For the oil-in-water emulsion type, when the wettability of nanoparticles does not change, the emulsion drop size would increase with an increase of nanoparticle size [20]. The smaller the size of emulsion droplets, the more stable the emulsions are [21]. Given that the wettability of nanoparticles could affect the emulsion type and stability [4], small sized carbon materials should be prioritized to realized emulsions with smaller droplet size and controllable colloidal behavior.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are kind of zero-dimensional nanomaterials, which are considered to have huge potential in a variety of applications such as catalysis, sensing, and storage [22]. Compared with two-dimensional GO counterparts, GQDs have some unique merits including smaller size, easy fabrication, more chemical active groups at the edge which makes GQDs easier to be functionalized, and fluorescent properties. However, pristine GQDs synthesized by bottom-up method showed poor emulsification behavior due to the excess hydrophilicity [5]. Wang et al. [19] synthesized functionalized GQDs via solvent thermal pyrolysis method, which were used in emulsion polymerization to synthesis polystyrene. However, the functional GQDs need to be at a high concentration (~2%) to stabilize styrene-in-water emulsion due to the amine group which limit the enhancement of hydrophobicity. Despite these prior efforts, the stimuli-responsive behavior of GQDs have rarely been explored in a systematically manner, and the colloidal behavior of surface-active GQDs at ultralow concentration is poorly understand, which may limit the large-scale applications of GQDs-based Pickering emulsifiers on an industrial level where the cost effective is of importance.

Herein, we report a Pickering emulsion stabilized by amphiphilic GQDs functionalized with dodecyl alkyl groups (C-GQDs) which are synthesized by a facile one-step method without solvent. The amphiphilicity could be acquired controllably and effectively. The C-GQDs based Pickering emulsion could be obtained with a very low concentration (0.001%), which could be utilized in an eco-friendly and cost-effective manner. Moreover, the demulsification took place quickly by adding a small amount of acid solution. C-GQDs could recover the emulsifying property by adjusting solution pH values. The reversible process of emulsification-demulsification could be switched readily several times by adjusting the pH value of the aqueous phase in the presence of C-GQDs. The as-prepared smart Pickering emulsifiers could be recycled to have potential applications such as enhanced oil recovery and oil/water separation.

Citric acid (CA, 98%) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. Dodecylamine (DA, 98%) was obtained from TCI Development Co., Ltd. Sodium chloride (NaCl, 99%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 99%), and sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Hydrochloric acid (HCl, 6 M aqueous solution) was obtained from Acron. Dodecane (≥99%) and dichloromethane (CH2Cl2, 99.5%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. All chemical regents and organic solvent were used as received.

In a typical procedure of C-GQDs synthesis, 1.4 g CA and 0.7 g DA were added into a vial, and then these two reactants were mixed by a vortex mixer. Then the mixture was reacted at 200 °C in air. After heating for 2.5 h, the brown crude product was obtained and cooled down to room temperature. The crude product was dissolved in CH2Cl2 to form a brown solution. The brown solution was then washed by HCl solution (0.01 M), NaHCO3 solution (5 wt%) and DI water respectively to remove unreacted regents in order to obtain purified products. For comparison, hydrophilic unmodified graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized according to a reported method [23]. Typically, 2 g CA was added into a 20 mL vial, and reacted at 200 °C for 2.5 h in air.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of C-GQDs based Pickering emulsion
ce:section.ce:para: For forming Pickering emulsion, dodecane and deionized (DI) water were chosen as oil phase and aqueous phase respectively. As-prepared C-GQDs were dissolved in DI water and the final concentration of C-GQDs in aqueous solution is 0.001%. The volume ratio of dodecane to DI water is 10%. Then the dispersion was sonicated by sonication tip (frequency ~ 20 kHz) for 30 s and left standing for 30 min, until stable oil-in-water emulsion was formed. To investigate GQDs forming Pickering emulsion behavior, the emulsion was prepared as the same method with C-GQDs-based Pickering emulsion.

The reversible pH-responsive behavior of the Pickering emulsion was investigated by adding HCl or NaOH aqueous solution to tune the pH values. The emulsion preparation was similar to the previous description. As-prepared aqueous solution with 0.001% C-GQDs and dodecane were homogenized by a vortex mixer for 40 s to form stable emulsion. The 20 µL of 0.1 M HCl (aq.) was added to the 5 mL of C-GQDs stabilized emulsions without shaking to observe if the emulsions broke up. The 20 µL of 0.1 M NaOH (aq.) was added to neutralize acid, followed by a vortex mixer for 40 s to form emulsion again. The demulsification and emulsification recycle performance were investigated by repeating the cycle five times. The image of emulsion droplets was investigated by an optical microscope.

The chemical structure was characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Thermo Nicolet 380) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Omicron’s DAR) using Mg Kα radiation as the excitation source. The morphologies of C-GQDs were investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2010, JEOL) and atomic force microscopy (AFM, Bruker Dimension Icon AFM). The performance of ultraviolet–visible spectrum (UV–Vis) and photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained by UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu 2550) and fluorescence spectrometer (PicoQuant FluoTime 100). The samples for UV–Vis and PL spectra were further purified by a dialysis tube with 2000 Da molecular weight cut-off. The sizes and zeta potential of C-GQDs were investigated by dynamic light scattering (DLS, Zetasiser Nano ZS90, Malvern Instruments) using 0.1% dispersion solution at room temperature. The surface wettability of samples was determined by sessile drop method. Homogeneous C-GQDs and GQDs films were prepared by depositing C-GQDs or GQDs dispersion (0.5 wt%) on pre-cleaned glass slide surface, followed by drying at 50 °C. The water-in-air contact angles of samples were evaluated by the sessile drop method. After cooling down to room temperature, 2 µL DI water was placed on the top of the film under an ambient atmosphere and measured after equilibrating for 20 s. The image of the water droplet on surface was taken by a high-resolution camera (Moticam 1000, Motic, San Antonio, TX, USA) and their contact angles were calculated by ImageJ software via Low-Bond Axisymmetric Drop Shape Analysis (LBADSA) with three replicates. The interfacial tension (IFT) between dodecane and modified GQDs with different ratios of dodecylamine and citric acid were investigated by tensiometer (Biolin Attention Tensiometer, Biolin Scienific, Gothenburg, Sweden) based on pendant drop method.

Citric acid was used as the only carbon source to prepare GQDs, which would make the graphene nanosheets contain abundant hydrophilic groups [23]. Due to these strong hydrophilic carboxyl and hydroxide groups, the unmodified GQDs could be completely wetted by water. As shown in Fig. 1 , the amphiphilic graphene quantum dots were prepared by introducing hydrophilic groups and hydrophobic groups (dodecyl alkyl chains) simultaneously. The chemical structures of C-GQDs were investigated by FTIR and XPS. As seen in Fig. 2 a, the absorption peak at 1690 cm−1 is assigned to -CO group [24]. Compared with GQDs, the strong peaks at 2851 cm−1 and 2923 cm−1 signify the presence of methylene groups, which demonstrates the introduction of hydrophobic hydrocarbon chains to the C-GQDs. The peaks at 3459 cm−1 present in the spectrum is assign to and OH stretching vibration, which are overlapped with NH [5]. These results indicate that hydrocarbon chains were functionalized.

The surface chemistry of C-GQDs was further studied by XPS (Fig. 2b-d). According to the XPS survey spectrum, C-GQDs mainly contained distinct peaks of C1s, N1s and O1s, on the contrary, GQDs spectrum only showed peaks of C 1 s and O 1 s. Based on the XPS data calculation, the nitrogen atom concentration is 4.47% in C-GQDs. In the C1s high-resolution XPS spectrum, the binding energy peaks located at 248.8 eV could represent sp2C-sp2C, indicating the graphitic structure [25]. The peaks centered at 285.8 eV can be assigned to CN, which indicates the existence of amide group formed by the carboxyl and amino group [19]. The peaks located at 287.3 eV and 289.3 eV represent CO and OC-O components respectively [22]. There are two peaks in the high-resolution O1s XPS spectrum, corresponding to –OH and OC groups [22]. These results prove that alkyl chains were functionalized on C-GQDs edge by amide bonds. To further prove the fabrication of graphene quantum dots, the UV–Vis and PL spectra were measured. As shown in Fig. S1, the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of C-GQDs is a broad peak which ranges from 350 nm to 650 nm. The amphiphilic C-GQDs emit blue fluorescence under 365 nm excitation. When the excitation wavelength is increased from 320 nm to 380 nm, the broad emission peaks are seemingly dependent on the excitation. This excitation-dependent PL behavior is common in fluorescent carbon materials. The PL intensity distribution can be explained by the effects of C-GQDs size distribution on the band gap [26].

The morphology of GQDs and C-GQDs was determined by TEM and AFM. For morphology of as-prepared C-GQDs investigated by TEM was shown in Fig. 3 a, the size of C-GQDs is distributed in a narrow range of 14–19 nm with an average diameter of 16.5 nm, which is similar with the size of GQDs (Fig. S2a). The thickness of C-GQDs was studied by AFM, as shown in the Fig. 3b. The height ranges from 2 to 6 nm which did not change too much compared with the thickness of GQDs (Fig. S2b), indicating that there are a few layers of produced C-GQDs [27]. The contact angle at water–air interface was measured to determine the wettability of C-GQDs. As shown in Fig. 3c, the contact angle of water for unmodified GQDs is 13.6°, suggesting high hydrophilicity due to carboxyl and hydroxyl groups [5]. The contact angle on the film of C-GQDs increases to 46.7°, which proves the introduction of an alkyl group to the graphene quantum dot surface. The results indicate effective improved amphiphilicity of C-GQDs, which strikes a balance between the hydrophobic group and hydrophilic group. The improved amphiphilicity is desirable to form stable Pickering emulsions.

The GQDs and C-GQDs were employed as stabilizer to prepare Pickering emulsion. At the low concentration (0.001%), GQDs were observed rarely emulsified as shown in Fig. S3, which due to its overwhelming hydrophilicity [5]. The amphiphilicity of nanoparticles was controlled by different ratios of two reactants. The graphene quantum dots modified with various ratios of alkyl groups exhibit different Pickering emulsion behavior, as shown in Fig. S4. With a high degree of modification of the alkyl groups, the nanoparticles have low efficiency of emulsification (Fig. S4 a-b). It is because there is an abundance of alkyl groups that stabilizer prefer to remain in the oil phase due to overwhelming hydrophobicity [5]. With decreased content of hydrophobic groups, the emulsion was developed and the oil droplets can be observed in microscope image, and the size of emulsion droplets increased from 20 µm to larger than 100 µm as shown in Fig. S4 c-e. This is because of a high charge of nanoparticles due to abundant unreacted carboxyl groups, which lead to strong repulsive inter-particle interaction [28]. The nanoparticles with improved amphiphilic properties prefer to assemble at the dodecane/water interface [19]. By introducing C-GQDs to aqueous phase, the IFT was greatly reduced from 55.4 ± 4.8 mN/m to 21.1 ± 1.9 mN/m (Fig. S5). Considering the emulsification efficiency and droplet size, the mass ratio of 2 for the ratio of CA and DA was employed for further emulsion behavior analysis. As shown in Fig. 4 b, the oil-in-water emulsion formed at very low concentration (0.001%). The average droplet size and droplet size distribution of dodecane-in-water emulsion was studied by DLS. The average droplet size of Pickering emulsion was 129.1 ± 14 nm. Meanwhile, the C-GQDs could be used to produce stable Pickering emulsions which did not change significantly after 72 h (Fig. S6). Moreover, it can be seen from a microscope image (Fig. 4a) that there are regular sphere oil droplets with relative uniform size distribution in water phase, in which all droplet sizes are less than 20 µm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reversible emulsion behavior induced by pH
ce:section.ce:para: For the emulsion stabilized by C-GQDs, the reversible protonation and deprotonation of C-GQDs provide an opportunity to tune the emulsion behavior by adjusting pH values. To study the pH-responsive behavior, the dodecane-in-water emulsion was initially made by the same methods described in Section 2.3. When the aqueous solution is adjusted to a pH value of 2, it remains two separated phases, as shown in Fig. 5 . When adjusting pH value of aqueous solution to high value (pH = 12), the size of emulsion droplet would be much larger than 100 µm and the emulsion is not stable (Fig. 5a). From the zeta potential investigated by DLS (Fig. 5c), the surface charged behavior of C-GQDs is dependent on solution pH values, which could affect the stability behavior in aqueous solution. When the solution pH value is 7, the zeta potential of C-GQDs is around −48.5 mV, which indicates that C-GQDs could be stable in the neutral aqueous solution. Under the neutral environment, C-GQDs contain both deprotonated and protonated carboxyl groups. Upon decreasing the pH of solution from 7 to 2, the abstract value of zeta potential of nanoparticles decreases significantly. When the aqueous solution pH value is 2, the zeta potential is around 2 mV, which is due to the complete protonation of the headgroup of carboxyl group in low pH solution [29]. This would lead to an aggregate of nanoparticles (Fig. 5b) due to the significant diminution of electrostatic repulsion between C-GQDs [8]. When the solution pH value increases from 7 to 12, the zeta potential of C-GQDs is still negative, which is due to the deprotonation of carboxyl groups to continue electrostatic repulsive interaction. Herein, surface charge and aggregation of C-GQDs could be controlled by adjusting solution pH values.

Based on the above pH-responsive performance, the pH-responsive reversible performance of C-GQDs based Pickering emulsion was investigated by the method as described. The process of demulsification-emulsification could be controlled by the protonation of the group on the surface of C-GQDs (Fig. 5c). When 20 µL of 0.1 M HCl aqueous solution was added into existing emulsion, the pH of the aqueous phase changed from neutral to acidic conditions. It was found that the emulsion disappeared rapidly without shaking (Fig. 6 a). If the pH value of solution was tuned back to neutral by adding NaOH aqueous solution, the two phases could form emulsion again after shaking. The self-assembly of C-GQDs at low pH conditions could be reversed readily by increasing the solution pH value back to neutral. The process of demulsification-emulsification induced by pH is reversible and can run at least five cycles. The repeated number of reversible cycles depends on the formation of ionic strength in aqueous solution during the acid-base circle (NaOH + HCl = NaCl + H2O) [16]. The increased ionic strength results in the unstable dispersions forming emulsion (as shown in Fig. S7) due to the shield of the electrostatic repulsive interaction between nanoparticles [16,29]. The zeta potential changes of C-GQDs after adding H+ or OH– each time were shown in Fig. S8. After five cycles, the zeta potential decreased a little to −38 mV, which might due to the salt screen effects [16,29]. Moreover, the C-GQDs could be recycled and reused to form Pickering emulsion with new oil in the system. As shown in Fig. 6b, when a stable emulsion was demulsified by adding H+, and then the pH value of aqueous solution was adjusted to neutral conditions by adding OH– but not homogenized. The separated upper initial oil phase was removed from the system and C-GQDs remained in the aqueous phase. After adding fresh dodecane phase, the residual aqueous phase could be used for further emulsification process to form a new Pickering emulsion. There are few effects on the C-GQDs concentration change by removing the oil phase. After such two cycles, the behavior of emulsification was unchanged, which indicating excellent recyclability. Therefore, the C-GQDs used as Pickering emulsifiers can be applied in sustainable operations due to the recyclability of the emulsifiers.

In summary, we presented a one-step synthesis method of amphiphilicity-reversing graphene quantum dots C-GQDs. We demonstrated that the amphiphilicity of C-GQDs could be on-demand controlled by the ratio of reactants. Compared with previous studies on Pickering emulsions stabilized by nanoparticles [6,18,19,30], C-GQDs could be used to stabilize Pickering emulsion at a very low concentration (0.001%). The Pickering emulsions stabilized by C-GQDs with responsive behavior was investigated, which demonstrated quick pH-responsive performance. The system could undergo multiple reversible demulsification-emulsification cycles by adjusting pH values of aqueous phase. It was ascribed that the surface charge and aggregation transitions of C-GQDs could be achieved repeatedly by changing pH values. Finally, the Pickering emulsifier could be recycled and reused in the emulsion system. These results indicate that the pH-switchable Pickering emulsion stabilized by C-GQDs with low concentration has great potential in many practical sustainable applications, such as enhanced oil recovery and oil/water separation.

Rong Ma: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Minxiang Zeng: Methodology, Validation. Dali Huang: Visualization, Validation, Writing - review & editing. Jenny Wang: Investigation. Zhengdong Cheng: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing. Qingsheng Wang: Project administration, Supervision, Resources, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The author Dr. Z. Cheng would like to acknowledge financial support from PetroChina Scientific Research and Technology Development Project. The authors acknowledge use of the Materials Characterization Facility (MCF) and Microscopy and Imaging Center (MIC) at Texas A&M University. The authors also thank Dr. Mustafa Akbulut for using DLS instrument.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2021.05.104.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

Nature

J. Colloid Interface Sci.

Angew. Chem. Int. Ed.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Pickering emulsions with controllable stability
Langmuir
